mnemonics.title: Process States Order
mnemonics.content: N-R-B-T: New → Ready → Blocked → Terminated (But Running is between Ready and Blocked)

mnemonics.title: PCB Components
mnemonics.content: PIRATE-S: Process ID, Registers, Accounting, Timer, Execution info, State, plus I/O status and Memory info make up the complete PCB

acronyms.usage: Data structure storing all process information
Devices and operations causing process to block
Unique ID assigned to each process
Selects next process for CPU execution
Handles process swapping in/out of memory

visualTips.visualization: Draw a circle for each state (New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated). Use arrows to show transitions: vertical for ready↔running, horizontal for blocked, downward for terminated. Label arrows with events (dispatch, preemption, I/O request, I/O complete, exit). This creates a clear visual map of all possible process lifecycle paths.

Imagine a restaurant: Ready queue is customers waiting for a table (CPU), I/O queues are customers waiting for specific orders (I/O devices). One head chef (CPU) serves one table at a time while multiple kitchen stations handle different orders.

Think of two people sharing one notebook. To switch users, you must copy what Person A wrote, erase, then let Person B write. The copying = saving context to PCB, the erasing = loading new context. This takes time and effort (overhead).

analogies.analogy: Think of a process like a student in a classroom: New = enrollment, Ready = sitting at desk waiting for teacher, Running = answering a question, Blocked = stuck on a problem waiting for teacher's help, Terminated = class dismissed.

CPU is like a checkout counter at a supermarket. The ready queue is the line of customers waiting. The scheduler decides who goes next. A customer making a payment (I/O) leaves the line temporarily and rejoins when done.

PCB is like a student's report card that tracks: who they are (PID), their current status (state), what they've completed (program counter = next page to read), their grades (CPU registers = work done), and their seat assignment (memory info). When they leave, their report card is stored.

Preemption is like a teacher taking away the chalk from a student who's been talking too long and giving it to another student who raised their hand. The first student goes back to sitting (Ready) and may speak again later.
